Heather Criss, CitY Administrator

Dorris, CA96023
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. ,4.-02-111
Dear Ms. Criss

QUESTION
CONCLUSION

1. You do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest based on your spouse's
employment uoO mu];*g" CiiV "fr..t, 
maa. payable to your husband's employer for
81000 - 91014
of Regulations.


File No. A-}z-lLl
Page No. 2
contracted work on City public works projects. The mere act of co-signing a check,
when your sole role in that regard is to veriSr the accuracy of the data and absence of
record keeping errors, does not involve you in making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision.2
2. The mere physical proximity of the bid documents, without more, cannot give rise to
a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act. A conflict of interest under the Act
arises, if at all, from the financial effects of a governmental decision upon an official's
economic interests.
FACTS
You are the city administrator for the City of Dorris ("City"). Your spouse is
employed as the operations manager for a conkacting firm located in Klamath Falls,
Oregon. In the past, this contractor has been the low bidder and successfully completed
road projects for the City. You anticipatethat the contractor will continue its long-
standing practice of bidding on City road projects. The City's bid process requires that
the bidder submit a sealed bid packet that is received in the city clerk's office, which is
also shared by your office. The bid documents remain under seal until being opened by
the city engineer or a member of the city council at a public meeting of the city council.
Once a project commences, the city engineer acts as inspector on the project. In
that role the city engineer verifies that performance is within project specifications and
approves progress payments to the contractor. All checks drawn on the City's account
for public works projects must be signed by three officials, which must include either you
or the city clerk. You are the last official to sign. Prior to signing checks you are
responsible for veriffing that there are no clerical or record keeping errors. You exercise
no discretion as to whether the City's payment will or will not be made or the timing of
that payment.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8)), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may occur whenever a public official makes a
goverlmental decision which may materially affect one or more of his or her economic
interests.
' You originally sought advice as to whether your spouse's employment created a conflict of
interest disqualifying you from any of your assigned job functions. Although you provided us a copy of the
city code sections describing generally the duties and obligations of the City's administrator, in the absence
ofa set offacts relating to a specific decision, this request is too general to elicit our advice. (Regulation
18329(b)(8), subdivisions (C) and (D), copy enclosed.) Thus, we restrict our advice to the two specific
questions that you clarified in your telephone conversation with Commission staff.


File No. A-02-ll1
Page No. 3
1. Are you a public official?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. A public
of{icial is defined as a "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
goverrlment agency...." (Section82048; regulation 18701(a).) As the city administrator
for the City of Dorris, you are an employee of the City and a public official within the
meaning of the Act.
,,
Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises or mal<es recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
In a telephone conversation with Commission stafi you clarified that your advice
request asks two specific questions: a) whether you have a conflict of interest
disqualifying you from signing checks issued to your spouse's employer for its
performance under City public works contracts; and b) whether physical delivery of
sealed bid documents submitted by your spouse's employer to the City offices which you
share with the city clerk creates of conflict of interest for you.
With respect to your first question, the facts you provide indicate that you
exercise no discretion with respect to whether public works checks are issued to the
contractor, nor do you exercise a role in determining when such a payment will be made.
Your sole role is that of verifying clerical accuracy and examining the underlying
paperwork for record keeping effors. In this, you are assuming a clerical role and
conducting a mechanical exercise not calling for use of your discretion or independent
judgment. You are not, therefore, making, participating in making, nor influencing a
govemmental decision when you co-sign City checks made payable to your spouse's
employer. (Regulation 18702.4(a)(I); Stausball Advice Letter No. 4-00-2 0l; Tripp
Advice Letter No. A-92-056.)
With respect to your second question, a conflict of interest under the Act arises, if
at all, in context of a public official's involvement in governmental decisions. The
physical proximity of bid documents to you is not, by itself, a govemment decision.
Thus, the physical proximity of these documents does not create a conflict of interest for
you.


/
File No. A-02-llI
Page No. 4
Since we determine that you are not making,participating in making, nor
influencing a govemmental decision in the context oithe questions you rais-e, it is
unnecessary to continue with the remaining steps of our standard eigfrrstep conflict of
interest analysis in order to advise you that youdo not have a conflitt of interest
disqualifying you from co-signing City checks made payable to your spouse,s employer.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By
Jtt^J-
Glick
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
KLGjg
